food_subgroup: Flatbread / wrap with processed meat and cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for piadina with beef pepperoni and hot honey. FOODON_00002572 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pizza and similar products) is the closest available category for a piadina-style flatbread with pepperoni and cheese. No dedicated piadina code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific commercial flatbread product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole piadina flatbread (approximately 200 g), as served at Now Pizza, Funan, Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pizza with meat, regular crust (SR Legacy #21299) and Pepperoni, beef (SR Legacy #07062), adapted for piadina flatbread format
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutritional entry exists for Hot Honey Beef Pepperoni Piadina in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA SR Legacy data for: (1) Italian flatbread / piadina (analogous to thin-crust pizza dough, USDA #21299 base), (2) beef pepperoni (USDA SR Legacy #07062), (3) mozzarella cheese (USDA #01026), (4) cheddar cheese (USDA #01009), and (5) honey (USDA #19296). Hot honey contribution is minor and treated as equivalent to plain honey with negligible chilli extract. Basil is garnish-level and nutritionally negligible. Proportions estimated as: flatbread 40%, beef pepperoni 20%, mozzarella 15%, cheddar 15%, honey 8%, basil 2%. Per-100g values derived from weighted blend of these components.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White bread / refined flatbread analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for pizza (thin crust, cheese and tomato, GI ~60) used as closest analogous product.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hot Honey Beef Pepperoni Piadina. The University of Sydney GI Database lists thin-crust cheese pizza at GI ~60 (medium). Piadina is a thin Italian flatbread made from refined wheat flour, structurally similar to thin-crust pizza base. The addition of beef pepperoni (high protein and fat) and cheese (high fat and protein) moderates the glycemic response relative to plain bread. Hot honey adds simple sugars but in modest quantity (~8% by weight). A GI of 57 is estimated, consistent with the pizza analogue and the protein/fat-mediated glycemic dampening effect documented in peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al.). GL per serving = (57 × 49.2 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 28.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g; ~1240 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish, as one serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium — over 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from beef pepperoni and processed cheese.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and moderate carbohydrate load with added honey sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this piadina in controlled portions, as the medium GI (57) and glycemic load of ~28 per serving, combined with added honey sugars (~6.8 g sugar per 100 g), can produce a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and monitoring portion size is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.9 g per 100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g), high sodium, and processed meat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this piadina due to its combination of saturated fat from processed cheese and beef pepperoni, detectable trans fat, and high sodium, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Hot Honey Beef Pepperoni Piadina is an Italian-inspired flatbread dish served at Now Pizza, Funan, Singapore. A piadina (piadina romagnola) is a traditional thin, unleavened Italian flatbread originating from the Emilia-Romagna region of Italy, made from refined wheat flour, lard or olive oil, salt, and water, then cooked on a flat griddle until lightly crisp. In this Singapore-market adaptation, the 10-inch flatbread is topped with smoky beef pepperoni slices, a blend of gooey mozzarella and cheddar cheeses, and finished with a drizzle of hot honey — a condiment combining floral honey with chilli heat — and fresh basil leaves. The result is a dish that balances sweet, spicy, savoury, and umami flavour profiles with a crispy-yet-chewy texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 268 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 13.8 g fat (of which 5.9 g is saturated), and 24.6 g carbohydrate with 6.8 g sugars. Sodium is notably high at ~620 mg per 100 g (~1240 mg per serving), driven by the cured beef pepperoni and processed cheeses. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 28 per serving. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, processed meat), and Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, added honey sugars). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without verification of the beef pepperoni source. The dish is best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary staple.
common_user_queries: Is the beef pepperoni piadina halal?
How many calories in a hot honey beef pepperoni piadina?
Is this dish suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of beef pepperoni flatbread?
Is piadina the same as pizza?
What cheese is used in the Now Pizza piadina?
Can I eat this if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of piadina?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this specific commercial product was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients (flatbread, beef pepperoni, mozzarella, cheddar, honey). GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for thin-crust cheese pizza. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry for this specific product was available. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if the restaurant publishes verified nutritional information. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the component sources used for estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on thin-crust cheese pizza (University of Sydney GI Database); the actual GI of this specific product has not been measured. 6. Halal status of the beef pepperoni used by Now Pizza - Funan has not been independently verified; consumers requiring halal certification should confirm with the restaurant directly. 7. Trans fat value of 0.2 g per 100 g is estimated from the beef pepperoni and cheese components; actual trans fat may vary by supplier. 8. The hot honey drizzle contributes simple sugars (fructose and glucose) which are reflected in the sugar_g estimate of 6.8 g per 100 g.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singapore-served dish (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Estimated per_100g nutrients via USDA SR Legacy component-weighted averaging with full provenance documentation.
Assigned GI of 57 (medium) using University of Sydney GI Database thin-crust pizza analogue with GL calculation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Selected FoodOn closest_parent and FoodEx2 closest_parent with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Confirmed gi_category = 'medium' is correct for GI = 57 (56-69 range per validator thresholds).
